aliases: มะพร้าวอ่อน

thai: มะพร้าวอ่อน

food_subgroup: Fresh coconut — whole young coconut served with coconut water and soft flesh

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301415 represents coconut (whole); used as closest parent as no specific 'young fresh coconut' term exists in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fresh coconut as a commodity; used as closest parent since no specific young/drinking coconut sub-code is available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229956000 represents coconut as a substance; used as closest parent as no specific young fresh coconut concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole young Thai coconut (approximately 450 g edible portion including coconut water and soft jelly flesh), typically served chilled with a straw and spoon.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut water, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170172); Coconut meat, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169120)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: A whole fresh young coconut as served consists predominantly of coconut water (~80% by edible weight) and soft jelly coconut flesh (~20%). Per-100g values are a weighted composite: 80% coconut water (USDA FDC 170172) and 20% raw coconut meat (USDA FDC 169120). This is the closest defensible approximation using approved sources; no direct 'whole young coconut' entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: Coconut water, GI value from peer-reviewed literature — Lunde et al. and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for coconut water
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for whole fresh young coconut. GI of 54 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for coconut water (GI ~54, low category), which constitutes the dominant glycemic component (~80% of edible weight). Soft coconut jelly flesh has negligible additional glycemic impact due to low carbohydrate density. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 26.1) / 100 ≈ 14.1, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 5.8 × 4.5 = 26.1g.

hypertension.guidance: Thai fresh coconut is low in sodium (~30 mg/100g) and provides meaningful potassium (~203 mg/100g), which supports a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio beneficial for blood pressure management; individuals on potassium-restricted diets due to renal complications of hypertension should monitor intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Natural sugars and glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although coconut water has a moderate GI (~54) and the whole coconut has a low-to-moderate glycemic load per serving (~14), the natural sugar content (~4.5g/100g, predominantly glucose and fructose) means that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and account for the carbohydrate contribution (~26g per whole coconut) within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high potassium, cholesterol-free
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Thai fresh coconut is cholesterol-free and low in total fat (~1.5g/100g), with the saturated fat content (~1.3g/100g) being modest at typical serving sizes; the potassium content may support vascular health, making it a generally heart-friendly beverage choice when consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Fresh Coconut (มะพร้าวสด, ma phrao sod) is a whole young coconut served chilled, typically with the top cut open to allow drinking of the naturally sweet coconut water inside, accompanied by a spoon to scoop out the soft, translucent jelly-like flesh (coconut meat). It is a popular street food and restaurant beverage across Thailand and Southeast Asia, widely available in Singapore at Thai restaurants such as Sanook Kitchen. The coconut water is mildly sweet, slightly nutty, and refreshing, while the soft flesh has a delicate, gelatinous texture and subtle coconut flavour. Nutritionally, a whole young coconut provides approximately 165 kcal per serving (450g), with natural sugars (~20g), modest carbohydrates (~26g), very low fat (~7g), and meaningful electrolytes including potassium (~914mg) and sodium (~135mg). The drink is naturally cholesterol-free, dairy-free, gluten-free, vegan, and halal. It has a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~54) and a glycemic load of approximately 14 per whole coconut. Clinically, it is a suitable hydration choice for most individuals, though those with Type 2 Diabetes should account for its natural sugar content. Its high potassium and low sodium profile make it beneficial for blood pressure management. Thai fresh coconut is also known as young coconut, fresh drinking coconut, or ma phrao on (มะพร้าวอ่อน) when referring specifically to the young variety.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a whole fresh coconut?
Is coconut water good for blood pressure?
What is the GI of coconut water?
How much sugar is in a fresh coconut?
Young coconut vs mature coconut nutrition

young coconut: มะพร้าวอ่อน
coconut water: น้ำมะพร้าว

confidence_reason: No direct 'whole young fresh coconut' entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for coconut water (FDC 170172) and raw coconut meat (FDC 169120) at an 80:20 ratio by edible weight, which is a defensible approximation. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for coconut water. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate (80% coconut water, 20% soft coconut flesh) based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries; no exact 'whole young coconut' entry was found in primary regional sources. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; coconuts contain no industrially produced trans fats. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero; coconuts are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; plant-based foods do not contain vitamin B12. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for coconut water or young coconut flesh in the USDA SR Legacy source. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for coconut water or coconut meat. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for coconut water. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for coconut water. 9. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for coconut water; no direct GI measurement for whole young coconut was found. 10. Glycemic load per serving = (54 × 26.1) / 100 ≈ 14.1, based on 450g serving with 5.8g carbohydrate per 100g. 11. The food_name has been normalised from the menu-style '906D Thai Fresh Coconut 新鲜椰子' to the canonical English name 'Thai Fresh Coconut'. 12. Chinese names (新鲜椰子 simplified, 新鮮椰子 traditional) are preserved from the input record. 13. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a Thai cuisine item, not a Singaporean dish; the mandatory multilingual requirement for Thai cuisine is Thai script only, which is satisfied by มะพร้าวสด.
